content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What's the scariest movie you've ever watched?

**Score:** 96
**Body:** I decided to go on a binge of that one article that measured the bpm of people while watching various horror movies list and it was fairly hit or miss. Some were properly horrifying like Smile and the first half of Sinister but others were just tropey snoozefests with scarcely anything scary like Host or Insidious. I was thinking I'd get some proper scares from y'all since the movie recommendations from here tend to be pretty good. I have no triggers so do your worst! Thank you so much for your recommendations in advance, happy halloween! I love you all<3🎃

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Jacob's Ladder" scared the heck out of me when I saw it in the theater years ago.
2. Event Horizon

The only movie where upon seeing bad things had transgressed where they were, the person in charge immediately says, "We're leaving."
  - Seconding this. It's one of only two movies that ever left me unable to sleep afterward, and I was an adult who saw it in the theater when it came out.

The other movie that scared me sleepless was Poltergeist, and that was on tape when I was nine.
3. The Others
4. "REC" is the one.
5. Session Nine
6. Threads
7. The Exorcist.
8. The Ring
9. I still rate The Descent. Claustrophobic, amazing sound design, and one hell of a jump scare (don’t watch the trailer cause they spoil it.) it’s pretty timeless as well. 

I find that the best horror for me is the ones that build tension and make me wince and then leave a lingering sting. Best example I’ve seen this year would be Bring Her Back.

I didn’t find Sinister scary. It had some effective moments but overall it was a bit meh, elevated by Ethan Hawke. Insidious as well was a great concept, but didn’t land overall for me.

## 2. Horror movie recommendation to watch with friends

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Hey guys, me and my friends are having a movie night. Can you please suggest a horror movie that is great to watch with friends? 

These are the movies we have already watched:

Blair Witch
As above so below
Gonjian: Haunted Asylum
Hereditary
Pope Exorcist




### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. try the weapons!! you and your friends will creep you out!
  - A hundred times Weapons. 
Starts dark and ends in a hilarious gore fest.
2. The Shining - best ever

Midsommar - same director as Hereditary

Cabin in the Woods - fun for a group

April Fool’s Day - 80’s retro fun for a group

Sleepaway Camp - 70s retro/campy fun for a group
3. Train to Busan

Wrong Turn (2003)

Drag me to Hell

Green Room

Don't Breathe
4. As Above So Below
5. The Descent is pretty intense.
6. Speak no evil (2024), Together, Heretic, The Boy. 4 movies that felt like perfect sleepover movies.

Out of them all, Together is the funnest to watch with friends and still freaky.
7. You’re Next was a crowd pleaser at a Halloween party we had some years ago!
8. Happy Death Day 1&2

Ready or Not

Freaky

Totally Killer

Tucker & Dale vs Evil
9. The Final Destination and Smile format is very fun to watch with friends.

## 3. Psychological thrillers that are horrors

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I don’t do well with horror (any level of gore is fine though), but I love a really intriguing, thought-provoking, psychological thriller- especially if it’s one that you can figure out the twist if you’re smart enough

Films like Shutter Island, Memento, Se7en, and Law Abiding Citizen

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Prestige 

Wind River 

Prisoners
2. Black Swan (2010)
3. The Skin I Live In
4. Silence of the Lambs

Manhunter (1986) and/or the remake Red Dragon (2002)

Frailty (2001)
5. Arlington Road
6. The Parallax View 
7. You might like **Gone Girl**, **Prisoners**, **The Others** or **The Sixth Sense** yow all more mind twistng than scary with great psycohlogical depth
8. Cure (1997)
9. Strange Darling (2023)
10. Identity (2003)

## 4. Films like Amélie, In the Mood for Love, La Notte

**Score:** 13
**Body:** looking for movies like Amélie (2001), La Notte (1961) and In the Mood for Love (2000)

I recently watched Decision to Leave (2022) and really enjoyed it. I appreciate the atypical/bittersweet relationship aspect 

Thank you for your suggestions

Bit of an honorable mention:
A Ghost Story (2017)
The Panic in Needle Park (1971) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fallen Angels also by Wong Kar-Wai
2. Roman Holiday
3. Lost in Translation (2003)
4. For the Panic rec, Straight Time.

Lola, Bay of Angels, What Do We See When We Look at the Sky?, Asako I & II, Queer, Lonesome, The Hand (WKW short), Never Let Me Go, Double Life of Veronique, Certified Copy, Bright Star, The Hole, Goodbye Dragon Inn, Mood Indigo, Once, Remains of the Day, The Age of Innocence, Two English Girls, The Lure, Blue, Le Notte Bianche, Four Nights of a Dreamer
5. Midnight in Paris!!!!
6. The Hotel Budapest.
7. Comrades: Almost a Love Story
8. Wings of Desire
9. My sassy girl
10. The Double Life of Veronique. Amelie was heavily influenced by it.

## 5. Looking for films where the leads are a mother & son. No babies, no kids, no teens. I mean a man in his 20s or 30s and his mother.

**Score:** 10
**Body:** To clarify, I mean the characters are mother and son. Not the actors. Can be from any genre or any year. Let me know your favorites! Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Psycho (1960)...
2. Dune
3. The Grifters (1990)
4. Stop! Or My Mom Will Shoot
5. Good Bye Lenin! (2003)
6. Mother (1996).

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mother\_(1996\_film)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mother_(1996_film))
7. Guilt Trip
8. "The Grifters" with John Cusack and Anjelica Huston. Very film noir.
9. Throw Momma from the Train is a classic.
10. Beau is Afraid

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Movies Where You Hate The Ending But Liked Everything Else?

**Score:** 878
**Body:** I'll start the Menu 2022

If you haven't seen this movie it's a thrill ride. Everything has meaning even the most useless details, and it's somewhat realistic.

The only issue is the ending I prefer it being ambiguous. I know the director (Mark Mylod) saying erm this is what I meant the movie to end like, final girl lives. Cookie cutter horror movie ending that ruins this. It's too boring especially in where a scene hints that their meat can be poisoned if not fermented right. Feels like the "happy  ending" a director puts because the screen testing didn't like it.

If you ignore that, it's fantastic.

No modern post credit scene of showing Columbo solving this, sadly.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved the ending on the second watch. She was observant of what made him love cooking. For me, she won the game. The menu is a performance restrained by the boundaries of kitchen/cooking discourse. 

Because she kept within the context of the performance, he allowed the improvisation, because she found a loop hole. 

Any cook would give someone food to take home if they asked. They love cooking for people, she reminded him of that before the final act. 

Just my interpretation so far!
  - Couldn't have said it better! I watched it twice and liked the 2nd watch more because i was able to pick up all the subtle hints and foreshadowing. The dialogue is very layered, so good!
2. Nahh loved the ending because she was not part of the plan. She evaded the noose by being observant and careful. Everyone else deserved their fate.
  - Yeah she was the only one Chef Slowik respected because she wasn’t stuck up and told it like it was. Her ordering a simple cheeseburger and enjoying it was better more authentic praise than any review or social media post about his food from the other patrons and they both knew it. He also understood that like his line chefs, she doesn’t get much respect for being a sex worker and are seen as beneath those they are serving.
    - She also gave him a moment of joy by actually making food for someone
    - The sex worker/working class dynamic just hit me. Thank you!
  - Yep. Didn’t feel unearned or Mary Sue writing. She genuinely makes good observations and didn’t deserve to die in the first place. The ending works for me
3. I liked all of The Menu, including the ending.

As for movies where I loved the ride, but hated the ending, Law Abiding Citizen is the first that comes to mind.

EDIT Adding in my personal perfect ending to Law Abiding Citizen from another reply below.

>!Nick discovers the bomb as in the final cut, and is able to save the people, but by the time he gets to Clyde's cell, Clyde knew that he discovered the bomb and has disappeared. Later, we see Clyde plant a bomb in Nick's car and wait for Nick to get in. Clyde sees Nick approach the car and is preparing to kill him when Nick's daughter runs up to the car to say goodbye. Clyde sees their love and disarms the bomb instead of detonating it. He leaves a voice message for Nick saying that he's forgoing his remaining vengeance in memory of his daughter and Nick's daughter. Optional stinger, we see Clyde, in disguise in another country see a headline of judicial injustice and close the newspaper.!<
4. Really? But the ending makes the movie....
  - Right.... the ending was kinda the whole point.

## 2. Why the hell Coen Brothers split up?

**Score:** 853
**Body:** The Coen Brothers are without a doubt the most famous and accomplished brother-director duo in cinema since the Marx Brothers. So why did they break up? After making so many great films over the past 40 years, they decided to direct separately. Since the split, the elder brother, Joel, made a self-serious adaptation of Shakespeare, while the younger brother, Ethan, directed a B-grade lesbian softcore film with his wife. For the sake of their fans, they really need to reunite and start making great movies again.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Well Dude, we just don’t know.
  - A lotta ins, lotta outs, lotta what-have-you’s
    - Well, that's just, like, your opinion, man.
2. They didn’t, they just did their own thing for awhile. They’re about to start filming a horror film soon.
  - That’s fucking interesting, man.
    - New shit has come to light, man
3. Ethan wanted to focus on lesbian tales.
  - The beaver picture?
4. I feel like these sibling filmmaker teams always eventually go their separate ways (the Hughes, The Wachowskis, more recently the Safdies) and it's kind of amazing that the Coens stayed together for as long as they did.
  - I mean, my brother is my best friend, but I can imagine if we worked together for 40+ years, we'd probably eventually want a break from each other.

## 3. In your opinion, what is the greatest sports film of the 21st century?

**Score:** 504

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moneyball
  - The rewatch-ability of Moneyball is pretty insane. I literally never get tired of it in the slightest and I’ve probably seen at least parts of it close to 10 times. Maybe more.
2. GOON
  - Two rules, man: stay away from my fuckin' percocets and do you have any fucking percocets?
3. Rush is so damn good. Another couple of my favs are Moneyball and Friday Night Lights.
  - Friday night lights is criminally underrated.
4. Miracle should be a choice too.
5. Rush is incredible.
6. Ford vs Ferrari is up there for me
7. Talladega Nights and no I’m not joking it’s a legitimately great sports movie.

## 4. In Fantastic Four first steps I can suspend my disbelief for people having superpowers but I cannot believe this speech would actually work

**Score:** 473
**Body:** First off, I just want to say I love the aesthetic of this movie it feels like live action Jetsons.

I want to be clear it's a nice speech ,there's nothing wrong with it, but it would not convince people, like the movie seems to imply it would. Maybe for some people, but not the ones who have gotten to the point where they're willing to riot outside and sacrifice a child. Now, I would believe it if this was after Reed had already figured out a plan to give the people, but this is before. I know this is a fantasy world and the weird thing to hold on to, but it's a part I just could not get past.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It wouldn't in our world.  But in their world - a world that isn't as cynical as ours and that they have saved and served countless times.  That has benefitted from Reed's inventions and have largely earned the trust of - yeah... i can see it working.

In our world no.
  - That's fair like I said it is a fantasy world it's just something I couldn't mentally get past
    - In the first 10 minutes of the movie they establish that, under the Future Foundation, almost every country on Earth dismantled their armed forces. They quite literally brought world peace. If you can buy into that, this should be no problem. 

I feel like a lot of people missed what was being said in that scene because the monkeys in our heads started clapping when the Latveria sign came on screen
    - That's why this movie is my favorite Marvel film. In my eyes, it brought a sense of hope back into superhero stories. The F4 weren't just beating up bad guys and destroying the city in the process. They all worked to better the world both as people and as heroes. The film argues that the population turning on them isn't because they are evil or selfish, but scared and feeling helpless. They are objectively good people who take their responsibilities as heroes seriously.
  - Exactly, that universe was more or less the MCU's equivalent of Earth One from DC, as in the classic silver age version of these heroes who are all wholesome and live in a much less dark timeline.
2. Yeah, you can tell that this is set in an alternate reality when the go back to Earth knowing that Galactus will spare them if they give up Franklin and then they... tell everybody about it without hesitation.
  - Worlds smartest man by the way xD
3. I assumed Doom would step in to take the child, which would've been a more appropriate intro for him. The world would never unanimously agree to teleport the planet
4. "We should love each other like a mother loves their child"

- terrorists, world leaders, police and criminals all see this speech at the same time.
- all drop their weapons, start making out with eachother
5. This is a utopian world where literally all their problems have been solved by the Fantastic Four. Every nation in the world abolished their military and they are so willing to engage in diplomacy that the Fantastic Four can ask them to mobilize and contribute material, time and manpower for their plan and also have the entire planet save energy and they just do it.

They trust the Fantastic Four and they're not us. That's why they wholeheartedly believed every word Sue said because they know they can count on her. The Fantastic Four aren't just politicians or scientists, they're men and women of action. They can back up what they promise and get shit done. The people were just afraid when their heroes buckled for the first time.

## 5. Films/Shows that drag you through hell and leave you with one last glimmer of hope.

**Score:** 408
**Body:** 
- True Detective S1
- Se7en
- Manchester By The Sea
- Before Midnight
- The Shawshank Redemption

Obviously there are different levels of hell based on the type of movie/show, but these are some that immediately come to mind. 

What are your picks? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fuck I forgot about Manchester by the Sea, the police station scene and the reunion with his ex wife are some of the saddest and best acted scenes in recent memory
  - The run in with Michelle Williams is heart breaking.
    - “My heart was broken, and it’s always gonna be broken, and I know yours is too” absolutely stellar delivery from her. I love how her character never finishes a sentence in that scene, it does a great job of showing the immense grief she still feels
2. Forrest Gump is excruciatingly painful to watch once youve seen the sequel Philadelphia and know that Forrest wont survive. It ruined the og and now I cant even eat boxes of chocolate without tearing up.
  - I usually don't cry. But fuck the police station scene and the bumping into the ex wife scene.... I felt it. I know Casey's a cunt but that was fucking fine acting from him and williams
3. I'd say The Road - its devastating and bleak, but that tiny spark of ;ove between father and son keeps you holding on
4. Don't know what *Shawshank* is doing here tho, the whole third act is hopeful!
  - Yeah Shawshank doesn’t leave you with a “glimmer of hope.”  All Red’s hopes are realized in the final scene!
5. Children of Men.
6. *Children of Men*.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Why did Darren Aronofsky's Caught Stealing come and go so quietly?

**Score:** 365
**Body:** I’m genuinely baffled by how Caught Stealing came and went without much noise. You’d think a Darren Aronofsky film starring Austin Butler and Zoë Kravitz would do better, especially a crime thriller this stylish and pulpy. But alas, that's the state of modern cinema. But audiences were also pretty lukewarm or unreceptive. Instead, it felt like a blip, even though it might be his most accessible and purely fun film in years. Obviously, blame it on Sony for refusing to give this one a festival bow and sticking it in the dog days of August. A director friend of mine who adores Aronofsky didn't even know he had a new film out this year. 

But the more I sat with it, the more it struck me that Caught Stealing isn’t just a mild detour for Aronofsky. It’s a film where his long-standing obsessions (sin, guilt, moral reckoning, divine punishment) collide with a real sense of play and fun. It’s Hitchcockian in precision and got a Coen-esque rhythm, which is a departure for him but still under his signature umbrella. 

What really stands out is how controlled it is beneath the chaos. Every twist and tonal shift feels deliberate, not indulgent. The humor never undercuts the tension. Aronofsky has often been accused of self-seriousness, but here, his control of tone is remarkable. 

What I also love is how tactile the film feels. 1998 New York isn’t treated like a nostalgia trap but a real, breathing city. The bars, the grime, the music, the fashion all pulse with life. Aronofsky doesn’t romanticize the era; he immerses you in it. 

It’s also one of the few recent films that really feels like it has movie stars again. Austin Butler channels that old-school charisma but grounds it with vulnerability. He’s magnetic without ever winking at the audience. Kravitz, meanwhile, gives my favorite performance of hers (although, hot take, I'm not a massive fan of hers). Together, they give the film an energy that reminds you why we go to the movies in the first place.

And yet, for all that, hardly anyone’s talking about it.

I would blame most of it on the marketing and the late-August release, but even those who caught (pun) it didn't really sing its praises that I believe are earned. Perhaps some viewers didn’t know what to make of a filmmaker known for Requiem for a Dream and The Whale delivering a crime comedy with a beating moral heart. And for cinephiles, one may summise that the heaviness of his recent films weighed on their decision not to seek this one out. 

I’d love to hear what others thought. Did Caught Stealing deserve more conversation? Do you see it as a minor work, or is it secretly one of his most confident films?

I wrote a full review, digging deeper into these questions and Aronofsky’s worldview in the film, if you want to check out my expanded thoughts, but put your thoughts down below!!  
[https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm\_campaign=post&utm\_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false](https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t think Darren Aronofksy committed enough to the bit, for this to be a truly memorable experience. It tries to create a Big Lebowski/After Hours type movie about a regular dude getting swept up in an increasingly bizarre series of circumstances, but it is nowhere near as witty and wild as either of those films.

As it is, it’s a fine time to spend at the movies, but nothing truly special.
  - Though I agree that there's definitely elements of The Big Lebowski and After Hours in the movie, to me it felt more like he was going for a turn-of-the-century Guy Ritchie/Danny Boyle style film. Shallow Grave, Trainspotting, Lock Stock, Snatch. In either case, he didn't nail it and the end-product feels unlike those movies as well as very un-Aronofsky
  - This is the answer. A big ol’ cinematic “meh”. And when you’ve come from the heights of where Arronofsky’s been and that stacked cast it just feels like a waste.
    - I’d describe it as a “feel-bad romp”. DA is probably my favorite living director, and this movie was a swing & a miss. His best movies get a career-best performance out of an actor that has untapped potential, and even that was missing here. (Austin Butler had had plenty of strong performances so he wasn’t really a contender for this stunt DA is so good at.)
2. Personally, I didn’t like the movie very much. But—aside from Pi—I prefer my Aronofsky big and boisterous. I’m the guy that likes Noah and The Fountain and Mother!

As far as the broader public, this kinda low budget, hardboiled noir is very successful on streaming. I don’t think it does as well in theaters anymore. The Nice Guys, which I love, was also a bomb, so I don’t think this response is new.
3. Probably bad marketing and a confusing trailer. The trailer made it look like there were a lot of comedic beats, and quirky characters. I have A-List so I took a chance on seeing it. I was not expecting how dark, and violent it was. The trailer made that initial mistaken confrontation in the hallway seem like it was played for laughs, yet in the movie he was beat so viciously he lost a kidney. The different factions looked like they were silly caricatures and were instead vicious killers. I enjoyed the movie, but man it was not what I expected at all.
4. Going to be a very controversial take but, I think that basically a lot of people who are big cinema fans have sort of lost a lot of love for Aronofsky.

Not including *Postcards From Earth*, the last three Aronofsky films (*Noah*, *mother!* and *The Whale*) all were fairly mixed received by folks. *mother!* in particular was pretty widely mocked for it's unpleasantness and incredibly on-the-nose storytelling. Even those who liked *The Whale* mostly only cared about it because of Brendan Fraser rather than the story and script, which again, was considered to be fairly cliche and unsubtle.
5. He’s entered his David O. Russell period. Movies with great casts, good scripts, and competent but bland execution, resulting in “fine” movies that become forgotten before you hit the parking lot.
  - I’m a pretty big Aronofsky hater, but I don’t think he’s fallen off as much as O. Russell, ‘Amsterdam’ was so atrocious, I didn’t believe a single scene and it had empty energy. O. Russell I think actually lost his touch like so many auteurs over time. He could prove me wrong but last three movies seem like a clear trajectory.
6. I didn’t see it, but to me it looked like DA doing his best impression of a Guy Richie film. In the end it looked like trodden territory. Perhaps it was genius and had a bad trailer, but I was really disappointed by Mother so put me in the ‘blip’ column.

## 2. Real immediate feeling after watching My Dinner with Andre

**Score:** 10
**Body:** This evening i felt somthing an uneasiness in my stomach, chest, head, in my whole body.
Every little sound, movement suddenly freaks me out.
This evening I really felt something.
Didn't like what I felt, but I felt something.

Andre would probably say it isn’t a coincidence that I feel like I’ve been spooked by the reality of reality
 on the 31st of October, huh.
Maybe it is, maybe it isn’t.


### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Before Cornel West became a goofy third party presidential candidate, I would really enjoy listening to him talk about music and literature. He connected all great works through what he called the "tragic blue note." So something like John Coltrane's A Love Supreme is working on the same level as James Joyce's Ulysses. He described reading Kafka's Metamorphosis as a feeling that the earth below your feet has turned to jelly and you've lost the ability to keep your footing. Sounds like you're experiencing something like that. My Dinner with Andre is definitely a serious work of art, sometimes they do that to ya.
  - I never heard that term before, but I feel the familiarity of that in my gut. It's something I've been fascinated by since I was a child, I paid attention wherever I found it. I always wanted to step off the path and pull aside the velvet curtain to look at what's behind this life... I'm going to look into that.
    - Sorry, I misremembered, it's been a long time since I watched him speak. It's not just tragic, but tragicomic. Here's a quick video where he talks a bit about it.

https://youtu.be/3EYK4p0Byfw

## 3. Ambiguity in Mulholland Drive (2001) vs. Enemy (2013)

**Score:** 5
**Body:** When done well, ambiguity can be one of cinema’s most powerful tools. It is the difference between a story we simply watch and one we actually feel. Ambiguity invites us to participate, to find meaning in what is left unsaid. When it fails, it does not mystify; it alienates.

Two films that show this contrast are *Mulholland Drive* (2001) and *Enemy* (2013). Both explore identity, doubling, and dreamlike logic, yet they produce completely different experiences.

In *Mulholland Drive*, Lynch uses confusion to bring us closer to the characters. The blurred timelines and shifting identities reflect Diane’s guilt and longing. Even when the story becomes incoherent, the emotions remain clear. The Club Silencio scene captures this perfectly: the illusion collapses, revealing the pain beneath the dream.

In *Enemy*, Villeneuve’s ambiguity feels more like a puzzle than an experience. The cold visuals and deliberate pacing create tension, but they keep us at a distance. The mystery feels closed off rather than open. By the end, I was intrigued but emotionally detached.

For me, that is the key difference. Lynch trusts the audience to feel through confusion. Villeneuve asks us to decode it.

I’d love to hear how others interpret these films. Do you find *Enemy* emotionally engaging or more like an intellectual exercise?

This post is part of a series of essays I’m working on called [The Art of Disclosure](https://fragmentsandfocus.com/2025/10/30/the-art-of-disclosure/), which explores how filmmakers use ambiguity, misdirection, and concealment to shape audience perception. I’d love to hear your thoughts before finishing the next part.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. i feel pretty much exactly the same as you. enemy feels to me like it attempts to antagonize its audience and acknowledge itself as a puzzle box mystery. mulholland drive is made with an unshakable confidence in its concept and feels like its content to be whatever it wants to be regardless of audience attachment.
2. I think it's just a different type of storytelling. And neither type is better than the other. Both are making me feel something, it's that the experiences are different. You might have a preference, so the emotional connection might be stronger for one movie than the other, but for someone else, the opposite might be true. 
It's like saying The Tree of Life is better than 2001 because it's more humane or something. But these are two movies operating on different gears, and this shouldn't decide which one's better.
  - Thank you for your input! I agree they operate on different storytelling modes, and that’s part of what made me want to compare them.

My goal wasn’t to rank one above the other, but to look at how each film uses a specific narrative device and how that affects the viewer’s experience. I chose two strong, technically sound films for that reason.

I personally prefer Mulholland Drive, and I explain why in the essay.
3. I’d echo the other comment here to say that there’s not a clear, necessary reason to compare the two films or use one as meter against which to judge the other.

Enemy is a mundane film. Its purpose, expressed by the director, was to be able to concentrate on directing actors more closely than he’d previously done, in a small scale film.

Club Silencio is pure cinema, a depiction of emotion which lands, which is communicated thoroughly, as you say, despite much confusion existing around its context. In fact, to me, I don’t necessarily know at all what the emotion of the scene ‘has to do with’, but it’s clearly a transcendent expression of cinema and its power to convey emotion.

Clearly Denis Villeneuve wasn’t trying to do something like this in Enemy. In Dune Part II, with the resources of a massive camera crew, a vast desert of rolling sand dunes at sunset, Hans Zimmer, when Chalamet and Zendaya kiss, I think that’s a bit more worth comparing to Lynch and Club Silencio.

More broadly, if you want to draw a thesis on the effects of ambiguity in film storytelling and a theory of how meaning is created and conveyed, I don’t think you’ll quite get there with such short paragraphs and declarative sentences like in this post, if I may say so.
  - That’s fair. My intent wasn’t to measure them against each other in scale or ambition, but to explore how both use ambiguity to express inner conflict. I see both as a study of the same emotion through different cinematic techniques.

The post here is just a condensed summary. I go into much more depth in the full article, where I break down how each film’s use of ambiguity shapes its emotional impact.

As for the writing style, I prefer direct sentences because I focus on clarity over academic framing. I’d rather let the ideas develop through contrast than through dense theory.

## 4. Themes in Back to the Future

**Score:** 0
**Body:** The main theme of Back to the Future concerns taking control and personal responsibility over one's destiny: A situation can be changed even if it seems otherwise impossible to overcome. Lea Thompson said the film represents how one moment can have a significant and lasting impact on a person's life. Bob Gale believed Doc provided the perfect summary of the series' running theme, when in Back to the Future Part III he said: "Your future is whatever you make it, so make it a good one."

At the start of the film, Marty is rejected at Battle of the Bands and admits he fears his ambitions will remain unrealized. He worries he will end up like his parents and sees direct evidence in 1955 of George, also afraid of rejection, and being unable to approach Lorraine; his fears risk Marty's future. Marty sets about manipulating the past to ensure his survival without concern for what impact his presence in 1955 is having on others. On his return to 1985, he is rewarded with wealthier parents and a nicer car, but he has simultaneously damaged Biff's future, reducing him to a valet for the McFlys. 

Despite rejection by film studios for not being raunchy enough, Back to the Future alludes to sexual assault and racial discrimination. The relationships between parents and children are the basis of many elements of the film. Lea Thompson believed the film had remained relevant to new generations because of its core idea that Marty's and the viewer's parents were once children and had the same dreams and ambitions they do. Critics favorably compared Back to the Future to It's a Wonderful Life, which offered a similar premise of a central character changing his future.

*Back to the Future* has been interpreted as an endorsement of Reagan-era policies concerning the American dream, self-reliance, initiative, and technological advancement. The Hill Valley of 1985 is depicted as run down and in decay, while in 1955 it is presented as a more simplistic and seemingly safer time, seen through a nostalgic lens. Marty's future is bettered because he goes back to 1955 and teaches George to be more assertive and self-reliant; his initiative leads to a more prosperous future for Marty with materialistic rewards. The film uses many brand names of the time, ostensibly to make the setting more realistic, e.g. Mountain Dew, Pepsi, and Texaco, but the filmmakers received financial compensation from the brand owners, making their inclusion promotional and commercialistic instead of artistic. 

The 1955 segment also presents a distorted view of America, showing an African-American band playing at the high school dance, which would have been disallowed. Similarly, the African-American character Goldie Wilson is seemingly inspired to work towards becoming mayor by Marty's intervention, inspiring a Reagan-style initiative and self-reliance. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I've never really interpreted it as a celebration of 1950s America and the "good old days" so much as a critique of that very Reagan-era nostalgia for it and how misguided that was. So much of it the 80s segments of the film focus on the parents lamenting the state of kids these days and how much more innocent things were when they were younger, yet all Marty's trip back to 1955 shows him is utterly full of shit his parents were.

Yes, 1955 Hill Valley certainly appears more clean on the surface when he first arrives there, but the further you get into the plot, the more apparent it is that his parents are remembering a heavily romanticized version of things that isn't really all that true. Just like Marty, both of them were deeply horny and desperate for sex, his dad in particular is kind of a straight-up pervert, his mother already seemed to be on the path to her later alcoholism, and Biff is a literal rapist. I think it does a pretty effective job at highlighting that all of the supposed greatness in America that Reagan wanted to get back to was really just surface level. Sure, maybe the streets were clean and the clocktower worked, but the people were the same.

It's still one of the most purely entertaining blockbusters ever made, but I do think there is a slightly cynical core to it that has made me appreciate it more with time.
2. >Back to the Future has been interpreted as an endorsement of Reagan-era policies concerning the American dream, self-reliance, initiative, and technological advancement. The Hill Valley of 1985 is depicted as run down and in decay, while in 1955 it is presented as a more simplistic and seemingly safer time, seen through a nostalgic lens.

I’m struggling to see how the film endorses Reagan-era policies. If anything, the run down state of Hill Valley in 1985 suggests a criticism of Reagan-era austerity as juxtaposed with Eisenhower-era high levels of government investment. 

>Marty's future is bettered because he goes back to 1955 and teaches George to be more assertive and self-reliant; his initiative leads to a more prosperous future for Marty with materialistic rewards. 

The interpretation of the film as a straightforward endorsement of assertiveness and self-reliance for material gain is heavily undercut by the sequel, where Biff uses underhanded tactics to turn himself into a Trump-esque casino magnate with gaudy wealth. While Marty’s certainly excited to have a new truck at the end of BTTF and his dad has a successful career, these material benefits are the cherry on top. The main benefits of Marty’s changes to the past are that his parents have a strong relationship, are in better shape, have clearly raised his siblings with more confidence, and support his relationship.

>The 1955 segment also presents a distorted view of America, showing an African-American band playing at the high school dance, which would have been disallowed.

Is it true that no predominantly white American high school in 1955 would have hired black entertainers?

## 5. Why do many people pretend that Horror films are better than ever? The Golden Age.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** In a 8 year span, we had Jaws, Alien, The Exorcist, Carrie, Halloween, Nosferatu, Invasion of the Body Snatchers, The Shining, The Omen, The Texas Chainsaw Massacre, Dawn of the Dead, Black Christmas, The Hills Have Eyes, The Wickerman, Suspiria, and a whole selection of other films.

Are you going to tell me that Megan 2.0 or Terrifier 3 or Weapons are at all comparable to these films I listed? What horror renaissance are people even talking about over the last decade? Has any horror film released in the last decade even anywhere near the level of some of the films I listed. I am dismayed at even drawing comparisons between horror films many decades apart, but what are people talking about when they mention right now is the golden age of horror.

3. I think the internet/social media is the reason.  Producers buy glowing reviews (cheaply) with swag or access, and after a decade or two people start to believe the noise.  “So many incredible horror movies out there, I hear about it all the time.”  

I think there ARE a lot of great recent films in this genre, but the formula(s) are so well known at this point and marketing has mastered how to go viral…so there are breathless raves about nearly everything that comes out.  People think it’s a Golden Age of Horror, but it may be more a Golden Age of Online Marketing.

5. I think the majority of horror ages badly.
